title: De Sole / The Book of the Sun — Marsilio Ficino
subtitle: The Book of the Sun (1494) — Ficino's final work, a theurgical manual disguised as natural philosophy, decoding the Sun as Īśvara tattva and theurgic ascent through light

summary.one_line: Ficino's De Sole (1494) is a theurgical manual disguised as natural philosophy: the Sun as the sensible image of the intelligible Good, the practice of ascending from the physical Sun (L₃) through ...
summary.scope: Covers all 5 chapters: (1) the prelude on light, (2) light as the image of goodness, (3) the Sun as lord and moderator, (4) the planets in relation to the Sun, (5) the power of the Sun in generation. Each chapter maps to a specific tattva range and upāya stage. The text is Ficino's most complete statement of solar theurgy.

sources.label: Marsilio Ficino — De Sole / The Book of the Sun (1494). Translated by Geoffrey Cornelius, Darby Costello et al. Full text in corpus at /root/projects/blog/scholars/theurgy/papers/de sol.md. 166 lines.

body.text: Chapter I: 'Oh magnanimous Piero, it is a truly divine Pythagorean precept that mysteries and things divine are not fit to be spoken about without light.' Ficino establishes the method: the Sun is the sensible image of the intelligible Good. Theurgic ascent moves from the physical Sun (L₃, the manifest light) through the celestial Sun (L₂, the planetary order) to the intellectual Sun (L₁, the Form of the Good) to the One (L₀, beyond light). The 'light' IS prakāśa — the illuminating power of consciousness itself.

Chapter II: 'Nothing recalls the nature of goodness more than light.' Ficino lists five properties of light: (1) pure and exalted, (2) radiated in an instant, (3) penetrates harmlessly, (4) carries nourishing warmth, (5) present everywhere yet mixed with nothing. These five properties map to the five pure tattvas (Śiva → Śuddhavidyā). Light IS the image of goodness because goodness IS the positivity that extends by its own nature. The 'nourishing warmth' IS prāṇa-śakti — the life-giving aspect of consciousness.

Chapters III-IV: The Sun 'rules and moderates all truly celestial things.' It is the ordering principle of the cosmos — the Demiurge, the 'This I Am.' This IS Īśvara tattva (4), the principle of objective manifestation that governs the descent from the pure path into the impure path. The planets correspond to the kañcukas: Saturn = niyati, Jupiter = kāla, Mars = rāga, Venus = vidyā, Mercury = kalā, Moon = māyā. The Sun stands at the center of all planets because Īśvara IS the organizing principle of the five coverings.

Chapter V: 'The Sun in its motion distinguishes days from nights and hours and months and years. Likewise by its light and warmth, it generates, quickens, moves, regenerates, fills with breath and cherishes all things.' This IS the Kālopāya (Tantraloka Āhnika 6) — time as the path of liberation. The Sun's annual motion through the zodiac IS the descent and ascent through the tattvas. The 'Part of Fortune' IS the karmic configuration of the jīva — the starting weights of the current incarnation. The 'daimon of the nativity' IS the navigator's attractor.

Ficino's practice: chant the Orphic hymns to the Sun at dawn. The hymn IS Mātṛkā activation through Greek phonemes. The practitioner: (1) chants aloud (vaikharī), (2) internalizes (madhyamā), (3) sees the Sun as light (paśyantī), (4) dissolves into the Sun (parā). The hymn IS a transformation of the Dirac operator — each phoneme is an eigenvalue, the sequence is the transformation. De Sole IS a complete upāya manual: Āṇavopāya (chanting, incense, planetary timing), Śāktopāya (contemplation of light), Śāmbhavopāya (becoming the Sun), Anupāya (the One beyond light).

gaps: Full Latin text not included; comparison with Ficino's Platonic Theology needed

issues.title: Latin text not extracted
issues.description: De Sole was written in Latin. The RO currently uses the English translation. The Latin text would allow phoneme-level analysis of the hymns.

versions.summary: Initial RO for Ficino's De Sole. 5 passages covering the theurgic method, light as goodness, the Sun as Īśvara, the Kālopāya, and the hymn as mantra practice.

timeline.event: RO created from Ficino's De Sole text. The Book of the Sun is now formally mapped to Īśvara tattva, the Kālopāya, and Mātṛkā activation.
